file_name: Liu et al. - 2024 - Claude 3 opus and ChatGPT with GPT-4 in dermoscopic image analysis for melanoma diagnosis Comparati.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used Large Language Models (LLMs) with image analysis capabilities, specifically Claude 3 Opus and ChatGPT with GPT-4 (GPT4-Vision), to analyze dermoscopic images for melanoma diagnosis. The models were prompted to provide differential diagnoses for 100 histopathology-confirmed dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive, and their diagnostic performance (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy) was evaluated as the primary results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is labeled as a 'Research Letter' and presents original experimental results comparing the diagnostic performance of Claude 3 Opus and ChatGPT with GPT-4 on 100 histopathology-confirmed dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive. It includes specific Methods, Results (including sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and p-values from McNemar tests), and Discussion sections, indicating it is an original study rather than a review.

title: Claude 3 Opus and ChatGPT With GPT-4 in Dermoscopic Image Analysis for Melanoma Diagnosis: Comparative Performance Analysis
brief_summary: This study compared the diagnostic performance of Claude 3 Opus and ChatGPT with GPT-4 in analyzing dermoscopic images for melanoma detection. The researchers randomly selected 100 histopathology-confirmed dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, comprising 50 malignant and 50 benign cases. Both models were prompted to provide the top three differential diagnoses for each image, and their performance was evaluated based on primary diagnosis accuracy, top-three accuracy, and malignancy discrimination ability using the McNemar test. Results showed no significant difference between the models in primary diagnosis or top-three accuracy, but Claude 3 Opus significantly outperformed ChatGPT in malignancy discrimination. The findings highlight the potential of large language models in assisting dermatologists while underscoring their current limitations and the need for rigorous clinical validation.

lesion_types_studied.details: 50 histopathology-confirmed malignant melanocytic lesions from the ISIC archive were used as the malignant class for diagnosis and malignancy discrimination.
50 histopathology-confirmed benign melanocytic lesions (benign nevi) from the ISIC archive were used as the benign class for diagnosis and malignancy discrimination.

all_task_types.target: Primary diagnosis (melanoma vs benign nevi) and malignancy discrimination (malignant vs benign).
all_task_types.details: The models were prompted to provide the top 3 differential diagnoses for dermoscopic images. Performance was evaluated based on primary diagnosis accuracy, top-3 differential diagnosis accuracy, and malignancy discrimination ability (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy). The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories (malignant melanoma or benign nevi) rather than continuous numeric outcomes.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive
datasets_used.description: A public archive of dermoscopic images. The study randomly selected 100 histopathology-confirmed dermoscopic images from this archive using a computer-generated randomization process to avoid selection bias.

use.testing: The 100 images were used as the test set to evaluate the diagnostic performance (primary diagnosis accuracy, top-3 differential diagnosis accuracy, and malignancy discrimination) of Claude 3 Opus and ChatGPT with GPT-4.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the diagnostic performance (primary diagnosis accuracy, top 3 differential diagnoses accuracy, and malignancy discrimination) between Claude 3 Opus and ChatGPT with GPT-4, as it is suitable for analyzing paired nominal data.
To estimate the uncertainty for sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and odds ratio metrics for both models.
To quantify the ability of Claude 3 Opus and ChatGPT to discriminate between malignant and benign conditions.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in diagnostic performance (primary diagnosis accuracy, top 3 differential diagnoses accuracy, or malignancy discrimination) between Claude 3 Opus and ChatGPT with GPT-4.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in diagnostic performance between Claude 3 Opus and ChatGPT with GPT-4.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: For primary diagnosis and top 3 differential diagnoses, the McNemar test showed no significant difference (P=.17 and P=.56, respectively), indicating the null hypothesis could not be rejected. For malignancy discrimination, the McNemar test showed a significant difference (P<.001), indicating that Claude 3 Opus significantly outperformed ChatGPT.

statistical_comparison.conclusion: No significant difference between the 2 models

ISIC_archive_100_images.notes: Both models made errors in diagnosing melanoma and benign lesions. Claude 3 Opus misdiagnosed several melanomas as benign lesions, while ChatGPT had a higher false positive rate, misclassifying many nevi as melanomas.

architecture_used.main_model: Claude 3 Opus (Anthropic) and ChatGPT with GPT-4 (GPT-4-Vision, OpenAI)

architecture_details.model_family: Large Language Models (LLMs) with integrated computer vision techniques for image analysis
architecture_details.capabilities: Image analysis capabilities enabled by integration of computer vision techniques into the underlying architecture of LLMs

training_details.training_method: Not applicable (models were used as pre-trained APIs for inference)

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Top 3 differential diagnoses ranked by likelihood
classification_or_output_head.prompt: Please provide the top 3 differential diagnoses for this dermoscopic image, ranked by likelihood. Focus on distinguishing between melanoma and benign nevi.

conclusions: Both Claude 3 Opus and ChatGPT with GPT-4 demonstrated potential in assisting dermatologists but also revealed significant limitations in dermoscopic image analysis for melanoma diagnosis.
There was no statistically significant difference between Claude 3 Opus and ChatGPT with GPT-4 in primary diagnosis accuracy (P=.17) or the accuracy of the top 3 differential diagnoses (P=.56).
Claude 3 Opus significantly outperformed ChatGPT in malignancy discrimination ability (P<.001), with an odds ratio of 3.951 compared to 0.616 for ChatGPT.

Both models made errors in diagnosing melanoma and benign lesions; specifically, Claude 3 Opus misdiagnosed several melanomas as benign lesions, while ChatGPT had a higher false positive rate by misclassifying many nevi as melanomas.
AI models, including LLMs, cannot yet replace the expertise of trained clinicians and require rigorous clinical validation before widespread implementation.

The findings underscore the need for developing robust, transparent, and clinically validated AI models through collaborative efforts between AI researchers, dermatologists, and other health care professionals.
Future research should focus on improving the robustness and interpretability of these models while addressing legal and ethical considerations for high-risk AI applications in healthcare.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small sample size of only 100 histopathology-confirmed dermoscopic images (50 malignant, 50 benign) from the ISIC archive, which may limit the generalizability of the findings.

Both models demonstrated significant diagnostic limitations, with Claude 3 Opus misdiagnosing several melanomas as benign lesions and ChatGPT exhibiting a high false positive rate by misclassifying many nevi as melanomas.
The models were evaluated using a single, fixed prompt ('Please provide the top 3 differential diagnoses...'), so results may not reflect performance with optimized or varied prompting strategies.
The study highlights that while LLMs show potential, they cannot yet replace the expertise of trained clinicians and require rigorous clinical validation before widespread implementation.

The research addresses legal and ethical considerations for 'high-risk' AI applications in healthcare, noting the need for proactive regulation and collaborative development between AI researchers and dermatologists.
